plainMeaning: Those whose only activity is bowing to you, O Lord, keep their wealth unrobbed and, with every obstacle cleared, swiftly reach the safe destination they longed for.
recitation: avilutta vihava sārā, tuha nāha paṇāma matta vāvārā. vavagaya vigghā sigghaṁ, pattā hiya icchiaṁ ṭhāṇaṁ.

wordByWord.gloss: unplundered, not looted (with wealth left intact)
wordByWord.etymology: Skt a- (negative) + vilupta, from vi-√lup, 'to plunder, rob'; Pkt vilutta
wordByWord.notes: Deliberately answers the looted, fallen caravans (paḍiya satthāsu) of the preceding verse.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt sāra, 'pith, essence, cream'; nom. pl. closing the bahuvrīhi that qualifies the travellers
wordByWord.notes: avilutta-vihava-sārā = 'those whose choicest wealth is unplundered'.

wordByWord.etymology: Pkt tuha, gen. sg. of tumha (Skt tava)
Skt nātha, from √nāth, 'lord, refuge'; vocative addressing Pārśva
Skt praṇāma, from pra-√nam, 'to bow down'

wordByWord.notes: Stresses that homage alone — no other effort — is the devotees' whole 'business'.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt vyāpāra, from vi-ā-√pṛ, 'activity, employment'; nom. pl.
wordByWord.notes: tuha nāha paṇāma-matta-vāvārā = 'whose sole occupation is homage to you, O Lord'.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt vyapagata, from vi-apa-√gam, 'to go off, depart'
Skt vighna; Pkt viggha; nom. pl.
Skt prāpta, from pra-√āp, 'to reach'; nom. pl. past participle
wordByWord.notes: The finite predicate of the sentence: 'they reached'.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt hita, 'set for one's good, beneficial'
wordByWord.notes: Qualifies ṭhāṇaṁ; a minority reading takes hiya = hṛdaya, 'heart's' — see notes.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt sthāna, from √sthā, 'to stand'; acc. sg.
wordByWord.notes: Primary sense: the journey's destination; by extension the longed-for spiritual abode.

literalTranslation: With the finest of their wealth unplundered, having homage to you, O Lord, as their only occupation — their obstacles gone — they have swiftly reached the beneficial, longed-for destination.
idiomaticTranslation: Their treasure untouched by any robber, busied with nothing but bowing to you, O Lord, every hindrance fallen away, they come quickly and safely to the blessed place of their desire.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: This is the 'turn' of the wilderness-robbery pair: the previous verse left travellers ambushed by Bhīls and bandits and cut down 'among the caravans' (paḍiya satthāsu), and verse 11 shows the opposite fate for the devotee. The poet works the contrast with one precise word — avilutta, 'un-looted' — set directly against that scene of plunder: where others' goods are seized, the devotee's vihava-sāra, the very cream of his wealth, passes through intact. The means is stated with disarming economy in paṇāma-matta-vāvārā: their 'whole business' (vāvāra = vyāpāra) is matta, 'merely / nothing but,' bowing. The single particle matta carries the verse's theology — no charm, escort, or ransom is invoked, only homage; the bow itself is the safe-conduct. From that flows a triad of results in quick succession: vavagaya-vigghā (obstacles removed), sigghaṁ (swiftly), pattā (reached). The closing object hiya icchiaṁ ṭhāṇaṁ is pointedly two-layered. On the surface ṭhāṇa is the traveller's destination, reached unrobbed; but hiya (hita, 'beneficial') tilts it upward — not merely the 'desired' place but the place that is genuinely good for the soul, the secure abode the devotee truly seeks. Thus the verse lets a newcomer hear a promise of safe arrival while an advanced reader hears the deeper sthāna, the goal of liberation, reached once the 'robbers' of obstruction are cleared by devotion alone.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The verse encodes the Jain conviction that bhakti's protective fruit is the removal of vighna (obstruction) — the same removal of hindrances sought across the protective stotras — but it locates the power not in any rite beyond the act of homage itself (paṇāma-matta). Read with hita-ṭhāṇa, it quietly reframes 'arrival': the truest unplundered 'wealth' is spiritual merit, which thieves cannot seize, and the 'beneficial place' is the soul's secure station on the path. Worldly deliverance from bandits becomes a figure for deliverance to the good destination.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: A reciter facing journeys, losses, or threats to livelihood takes this verse as assurance that single-minded reverence — not anxiety or stratagem — is the real safeguard; and inwardly, that guarding devotion keeps one's deeper 'wealth' of merit un-looted and carries one swiftly toward the goal.

crossReferences.text: Preceding verse 10 (aḍavīsu bhilla takkara… paḍiya satthāsu) — the robbery scene this verse answers
Verse 18 (the summary listing thief/foe — takkara — among the great fears stilled by Pārśva's name)

sources.consultedFor: Sanskrit etyma for the Prakrit tokens: vibhava, sāra, praṇāma, mātra, vyāpāra, vyapagata, vighna, śīghra, prāpta, hita, sthāna

notes: Verse text follows the Anchalgachchha/Vidhipaksha book verbatim, including spellings (વાવારા, વિગ્ઘા, ઠાણં). Key parsings: matta is read as Skt mātra ('mere/only'), not matta ('intoxicated'), which gives paṇāma-matta-vāvārā its force; sārā is taken as the close of a bahuvrīhi qualifying the (plural) travellers. hiya is read as hita ('beneficial'); a minority tradition reads it as hṛdaya ('heart's-desired place') — both yield a similar devotional sense. ṭhāṇa is intentionally double-valued (worldly destination / spiritual abode); the commentary preserves the ambiguity rather than forcing one. Other recensions may vary in conjuncts and mātrās; per the knowledge base, the book's readings are authoritative here.
